content:
# Genesis Chapter 31 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 17v–18v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_031_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_031_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_031_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 27–29 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0031

---

**1** Now Jacob heard the words of Laban's sons, saying, "Jacob has taken all that was our father's, and from what was our father's he has gained all this wealth."

**2** And Jacob saw the face of Laban, and behold, it was not toward him as before.

**3** Then the LORD said to Jacob, "Return to the land of your fathers and to your kindred, and I will be with you."

**4** So Jacob sent and called Rachel and Leah into the field, to his flock,

**5** and said to them, "I see that your father's face is not toward me as before. But the God of my father has been with me.

**6** You yourselves know that with all my strength I have served your father.

**7** Yet your father has cheated me and changed my wages ten times. But God did not permit him to harm me.

**8** If he said thus, 'The speckled shall be your wages,' then all the flock bore speckled; and if he said thus, 'The striped shall be your wages,' then all the flock bore striped.

**9** Thus God has taken away the livestock of your father and given them to me.

**10** In the breeding season of the flock I lifted my eyes and saw in a dream, and behold, the male goats that mounted the flock were striped, speckled, and spotted.

**11** Then the angel of God said to me in the dream, 'Jacob,' and I said, 'Here I am.'

**12** And he said, 'Lift your eyes and see: all the male goats that mount the flock are striped, speckled, and spotted, for I have seen all that Laban is doing to you.

**13** I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a pillar and where you made a vow to me. Now arise, go out from this land and return to the land of your kindred.'"

**14** Then Rachel and Leah answered and said to him, "Is there still any portion or inheritance for us in our father's house?

**15** Are we not regarded by him as foreigners? For he has sold us and has also entirely consumed our purchase price.

**16** For all the wealth that God has taken away from our father belongs to us and to our children. Now then, whatever God has said to you, do it."

**17** So Jacob arose and set his sons and his wives on camels.

**18** And he drove away all his livestock and all his property that he had gained, his acquired livestock that he had gained in Paddan-aram, to go to his father Isaac in the land of Canaan.

**19** Now Laban had gone to shear his sheep, and Rachel stole the teraphim that belonged to her father.

**20** And Jacob stole the heart of Laban the Aramean, in that he did not tell him that he was fleeing.

**21** So he fled with all that he had; he arose and crossed the River and set his face toward the hill country of Gilead.

**22** On the third day, Laban was told that Jacob had fled.

**23** So he took his kinsmen with him and pursued him a journey of seven days, and he overtook him in the hill country of Gilead.

**24** But God came to Laban the Aramean in a dream by night and said to him, "Take heed to yourself lest you speak to Jacob either good or bad."

**25** And Laban overtook Jacob. Now Jacob had pitched his tent in the hill country, and Laban with his kinsmen pitched in the hill country of Gilead.

**26** And Laban said to Jacob, "What have you done, that you have stolen my heart and carried away my daughters like captives taken by the sword?

**27** Why did you flee secretly and steal away from me, and did not tell me? For I would have sent you away with joy and with songs, with tambourine and with lyre.

**28** And you did not allow me to kiss my sons and my daughters farewell. Now you have acted foolishly.

**29** It is in my power to do you harm. But the God of your father spoke to me last night, saying, 'Take heed to yourself lest you speak to Jacob either good or bad.'

**30** And now you have indeed gone away because you longed greatly for your father's house, but why did you steal my gods?"

**31** Jacob answered and said to Laban, "Because I was afraid, for I thought, 'Perhaps you would take your daughters from me by force.'

**32** With whomever you find your gods, he shall not live. In the presence of our kinsmen, identify what is yours with me and take it." For Jacob did not know that Rachel had stolen them.

**33** So Laban went into Jacob's tent and into Leah's tent and into the tent of the two female servants, but he did not find them. And he came out of Leah's tent and entered Rachel's tent.

**34** Now Rachel had taken the teraphim and put them in the camel's saddle and sat on them. And Laban searched all the tent but did not find them.

**35** And she said to her father, "Let not my lord be angry that I am unable to rise before you, for the manner of women is upon me." So he searched but did not find the teraphim.

**36** Then Jacob was angry and quarreled with Laban. Jacob answered and said to Laban, "What is my offense? What is my sin, that you have hotly pursued me?

**37** Although you have searched all my goods, what have you found of all your household goods? Set it here before my kinsmen and your kinsmen, that they may decide between us two.

**38** These twenty years I have been with you. Your ewes and your female goats have not miscarried, and I have not eaten the rams of your flocks.

**39** What was torn by wild beasts I did not bring to you. I bore the loss of it myself. From my hand you required it, whether stolen by day or stolen by night.

**40** There I was: by day the heat consumed me, and the cold by night, and my sleep fled from my eyes.

**41** These twenty years I have been in your house. I served you fourteen years for your two daughters and six years for your flock, and you have changed my wages ten times.

**42** If the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac, had not been with me, surely now you would have sent me away empty-handed. God has seen my affliction and the toil of my hands and rebuked you last night."

**43** Then Laban answered and said to Jacob, "The daughters are my daughters, the sons are my sons, the flocks are my flocks, and all that you see is mine. But what can I do this day for these my daughters or for their children whom they have borne?

**44** Come now, let us make a covenant, you and I, and let it be a witness between you and me."

**45** So Jacob took a stone and set it up as a pillar.

**46** And Jacob said to his kinsmen, "Gather stones." And they took stones and made a heap, and they ate there by the heap.

**47** Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha, but Jacob called it Galeed.

**48** Laban said, "This heap is a witness between you and me today." Therefore its name was called Galeed,

**49** and Mizpah, for he said, "The LORD watch between you and me, when we are hidden from one another.

**50** If you afflict my daughters, or if you take wives besides my daughters, although no man is with us, see, God is witness between you and me."

**51** Then Laban said to Jacob, "See this heap and see the pillar, which I have set between you and me.

**52** This heap is a witness and the pillar is a witness, that I will not pass over this heap to you, and you will not pass over this heap and this pillar to me, to do harm.

**53** The God of Abraham and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge between us." So Jacob swore by the Fear of his father Isaac,

**54** and Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain and called his kinsmen to eat bread. And they ate bread and spent the night on the mountain.

**55** Early in the morning Laban arose and kissed his grandchildren and his daughters and blessed them. Then Laban departed and returned to his place.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of six manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 31 spans Folios 17v–18v across three pages.

   **Folio 17v** (GEN_031_page_001.jpg): The verso of Folio 17, presented in inverted orientation per WSRP imaging convention. This is the same folio page that concluded Genesis 30 (the selective breeding summary, 30:39–43). The Genesis 31 material begins clearly after a paragraph break visible in the right column and runs through approximately verse 19: Laban's sons' complaint and Jacob's changed relationship with Laban (vv. 1–2), the LORD's command to return (v. 3), Jacob's summons of Rachel and Leah and his speech about Laban's dishonesty (vv. 4–13), Rachel and Leah's response consenting to the departure (vv. 14–16), Jacob's preparations and departure (vv. 17–18), and Rachel's theft of the teraphim (v. 19). The three-column layout continues with Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks clearly legible throughout. Masorah parva is visible in the inter-column margins.

   **Folio 18r** (GEN_031_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 18, with the folio number "18" visible in the upper margin. This folio carries approximately Genesis 31:20 through 31:42: Jacob's flight (vv. 20–21), Laban learning of the flight and pursuing (vv. 22–23), God's warning to Laban in a dream (v. 24), Laban overtaking Jacob (v. 25), Laban's accusation regarding the stolen daughters and gods (vv. 26–30), Jacob's reply and his unwitting death sentence for the thief (vv. 31–32), the search through the tents (vv. 33–35), Jacob's furious speech enumerating his faithful service (vv. 36–42). The text is clear and legible throughout with characteristic Masoretic pointing and te'amim.

   **Folio 18v** (GEN_031_page_003.jpg): The verso of Folio 18, again in inverted orientation. This page carries Genesis 31:43 through 31:55 and continues into the opening of Genesis 32. The material covers: Laban's final assertion of ownership (v. 43), the proposal to make a covenant (v. 44), the stone pillar and heap of stones (vv. 45–46), the bilingual naming — Jegar-sahadutha in Aramaic, Galeed in Hebrew (v. 47), the explanation of Galeed and the addition of the Mizpah name (vv. 48–49), Laban's stipulations regarding Jacob's treatment of his daughters (vv. 50–52), the oath invoking the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor (v. 53), the covenant meal (v. 54), and Laban's farewell blessing and departure (v. 55). The chapter ends clearly, followed by the opening of Genesis 32.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Three pages were read directly.

   **Page 27** (GEN_031_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 27 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0027). The chapter marker "CAP. XXXIII" is visible in the left margin at the top of the page's material (continuing from Genesis 30). The Genesis 31 LXX text begins on this page, covering the opening section of the chapter through approximately verse 13. The continuous uncial Greek text is legible throughout in three columns.

   **Page 28** (GEN_031_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 28 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0032). This page carries the middle portion of Genesis 31, from approximately verse 14 through verse 44. The marker "CAP. XXXV" is visible at the right margin. The text covers Rachel and Leah's consent, Jacob's flight, Laban's pursuit, the dream warning, the confrontation and tent search, and Jacob's speech defending his service, continuing through the initial proposal of a covenant. Text remains legible throughout.

   **Page 29** (GEN_031_vaticanus_page_003.jpg): Vaticanus page 29 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0033). The marker "CAP. XXXVI" is visible at the top. This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 31 from approximately verse 45 through verse 55 and then continues into the opening of Genesis 32. The Mizpah covenant, the bilingual naming, the oaths, the covenant meal, and Laban's departure are all present.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "The face of Laban" — פְּנֵי לָבָן (vv. 2, 5)**

The Hebrew idiom פָּנִים (panim, "face") is used idiomatically throughout this chapter. In verse 2, Jacob "saw the face of Laban, and behold, it was not toward him as before" (וְהִנֵּה אֵינֶנּוּ עִמּוֹ כִּתְמֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם). In verse 5 he repeats the same idiom to his wives. The formal translation preserves "face" (not "attitude" or "expression") because this idiom recurs throughout the chapter and is part of its deliberate rhetorical patterning — Laban's face turned away from Jacob mirrors the divine face turned toward Jacob. The LXX renders πρόσωπον (prosopon — "face"), consistent with the MT.

**2. "The God of my father" — אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי (v. 5)**

Jacob throughout this speech uses the formulation "the God of my father" (Elohei avi) rather than the covenant name YHWH. This is significant: in verse 3, the narrator uses YHWH for the divine speech; but Jacob himself, speaking to Rachel and Leah, uses "the God of my father." The distinction is preserved in the formal translation. Jacob invokes God through the ancestral relationship — the God who has been proven faithful to his forebears — rather than making a direct covenant claim. The LXX renders ὁ θεὸς τοῦ πατρός μου (ho theos tou patros mou — "the God of my father"), consistent with the MT.

**3. "Changed my wages ten times" — וַיַּחֲלֵף אֶת מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים (vv. 7, 41)**

The phrase עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים (aseret monim — "ten times / ten counts") uses the noun מֹנֶה (moneh, "count, number, time"). The number ten here is likely idiomatic for "many times" or "repeatedly" rather than a strict count of ten discrete changes, in the same way that "ten times" functions idiomatically in Numbers 14:22 where Israel tested God "ten times" in the wilderness. The formal translation renders "ten times" — preserving the literal number while allowing the reader to perceive its idiomatic force — rather than paraphrasing as "again and again." The LXX renders δέκα ἀμνάδας (deka amnadas — "ten female lambs") on both occurrences, a strikingly different reading that apparently understands מֹנִים as a livestock term rather than a numeral multiplier. The formal translation follows the MT throughout.

**4. The Dream Vision — the angel of God (vv. 10–13)**

Verses 10–12 report Jacob's dream in which he sees the striped, speckled, and spotted male goats mounting the flock — the divine explanation of the breeding success from Genesis 30. In verse 11, the speaker is "the angel of God" (מַלְאַךְ הָאֱלֹהִים, malakh ha-Elohim), but in verse 13 the same voice says "I am the God of Bethel" (אָנֹכִי הָאֵל בֵּית אֵל, anokhi ha-El Bet-El). This identification of the angel of God with God himself — or the angel as the direct voice of God — is theologically significant and is a recurring pattern in Genesis (cf. 16:7–13; 22:11–12). The formal translation renders both designations as they appear: "the angel of God" in verse 11 and "I am the God of Bethel" in verse 13, preserving the theological complexity rather than harmonizing. The LXX renders ὁ ἄγγελος τοῦ θεοῦ (ho angelos tou theou — "the angel of God") in verse 11, consistent with the MT.

**5. "The God of Bethel" — הָאֵל בֵּית אֵל (v. 13)**

The divine self-identification אָנֹכִי הָאֵל בֵּית אֵל (anokhi ha-El Bet-El — "I am the God of Bethel") uses the specific divine designation הָאֵל (ha-El, "the God" / "the El") rather than YHWH or Elohim. This may be a deliberate archaizing — El was the chief deity of the Canaanite pantheon, and Bethel (בֵּית אֵל, "house of God/El") was the site of Jacob's pillar-vow in Genesis 28. The formal translation renders "the God of Bethel" — using the English article "the" to convey the specificity of ha-El. The LXX renders ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ θεὸς ὁ ὀφθείς σοι ἐν τόπῳ θεοῦ (egō eimi ho theos ho ophtheis soi en topō theou — "I am the God who appeared to you in the place of God"), which expands the MT's terse "God of Bethel" into a fuller relative clause identifying the theophany at Bethel.

**6. "The teraphim" — הַתְּרָפִים (vv. 19, 34, 35)**

The word תְּרָפִים (teraphim) appears three times in Genesis 31. The term is never fully explained in the Hebrew Bible; it refers to some form of household cult objects or figurines, possibly ancestor images used for divination or as talismans of household protection and inheritance rights. Their significance in this episode is debated: some scholars argue that possession of the teraphim carried legal significance for inheritance claims (based on Nuzi parallels), which would explain Rachel's motive for stealing them; others see the theft as simply an act of religious vandalism or protection against Laban's ability to divine Jacob's whereabouts. The formal translation renders "teraphim" throughout — retaining the Hebrew loanword as a technical term, following the practice of most formal English translations. To render it "household gods" or "idols" would import an interpretive judgment about their nature. The LXX renders τὰ εἴδωλα (ta eidōla — "the idols") in verses 19 and 34, and τὰ εἴδωλα in verse 35, treating the teraphim as simply idols. Laban himself calls them אֱלֹהַי (elohai — "my gods") in verse 30 — the formal translation renders this "my gods" to preserve Laban's own characterization.

**7. "Jacob stole the heart of Laban" — וַיִּגְנֹב יַעֲקֹב אֶת לֵב לָבָן (v. 20)**

The Hebrew idiom גָּנַב לֵב (ganav lev — literally "to steal the heart") means "to deceive, to act deceptively without the other's knowledge." In the same verse the Aramean identity of Laban is specified: לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי (Lavan ha-Arami — "Laban the Aramean"). The formal translation renders "Jacob stole the heart of Laban the Aramean, in that he did not tell him that he was fleeing" — preserving the idiom's literal form and explaining it contextually, as the Hebrew itself does in the following clause. The LXX renders ἔκρυψεν δὲ Ιακωβ Λαβαν τὸν Σύρον (ekrypsen de Iakob Laban ton Syron — "Jacob hid from Laban the Syrian") — the LXX uses Σύρος (Syros — "Syrian") for Aramean throughout Genesis, reflecting the Greek identification of Aram with Syria.

**8. "Crossed the River" — וַיַּעֲבֹר אֶת הַנָּהָר (v. 21)**

The definite article in הַנָּהָר (ha-nahar — "the River") with no further specification indicates a known landmark: the Euphrates River, "the River" par excellence in the ancient Near East (cf. Genesis 15:18; 2 Samuel 10:16; 1 Kings 4:21). The formal translation renders "crossed the River" with capital R to indicate this is the proper designation for the Euphrates. The LXX renders τὸν ποταμόν (ton potamon — "the river"), consistent with the MT's use of the definite article.

**9. "Speak to Jacob either good or bad" — מִדַּבֵּר עִם יַעֲקֹב מִטּוֹב עַד רָע (vv. 24, 29)**

God's warning to Laban uses the merism מִטּוֹב עַד רָע (mittov ad-ra' — "from good to bad / neither good nor bad"). In Hebrew idiom, a merism expresses totality: "from good to bad" means "anything whatsoever" — God is telling Laban not to say a single word to Jacob in either direction. The formal translation renders "either good or bad," following the established formal equivalent tradition (cf. ESV, NASB), which captures the merism's meaning while preserving its structural parallelism. The LXX renders ἀπὸ τοῦ καλοῦ ἕως τοῦ πονηροῦ (apo tou kalou heōs tou ponērou — "from the good to the evil"), consistent with the MT.

**10. "Like captives taken by the sword" — כִּשְׁבֻיֹת חָרֶב (v. 26)**

Laban's accusation that Jacob "carried away my daughters like captives taken by the sword" (כִּשְׁבֻיֹת חָרֶב, kishvuyot kharev) is a striking simile. The formal translation preserves it literally: "like captives taken by the sword." This is a morally ambiguous moment — Laban's rhetorical claim of victimhood must be read against the context of his repeated exploitation of Jacob and his daughters. The LXX renders ὡς αἰχμαλώτιδας μαχαίρᾳ (hōs aichmalōtidas makhaira — "as captives by the sword"), consistent with the MT.

**11. "The Fear of Isaac" — פַּחַד יִצְחָק (vv. 42, 53)**

The divine epithet פַּחַד יִצְחָק (Pachad Yitschaq — "the Fear of Isaac") is one of the most debated phrases in all of Genesis. The word פַּחַד (pachad) normally means "fear, terror, dread." As a divine title, it would mean "the one whom Isaac fears" or "the Terror of Isaac" — the awe-inspiring God before whom Isaac trembled. A minority scholarly reading connects פַּחַד to an alternative root meaning "kinsman" (comparing Arabic usage), yielding "the Kinsman of Isaac." The formal translation renders "the Fear of Isaac" on both occurrences — following the standard lexical meaning of pachad, well-established in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Isaiah 8:13 — "him you shall regard as holy; let him be your fear [pachad] and let him be your dread"), and following the established formal equivalent tradition. The LXX renders τὸν φόβον Ισαακ (ton phobon Isaak — "the Fear of Isaac") — φόβος (phobos — "fear, terror") — consistent with the MT's standard reading.

**12. The Bilingual Naming — Jegar-sahadutha and Galeed (v. 47)**

Verse 47 records a notable linguistic moment: Laban names the heap of stones יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא (Yegar-sahadutha) in Aramaic, while Jacob names the same heap גַּלְעֵד (Galeed) in Hebrew. Both names mean "heap of witness" — יְגַר (yegar) is Aramaic for "heap" and שָׂהֲדוּתָא (sahadutha) is Aramaic for "witness"; גַּל (gal) is Hebrew for "heap" and עֵד (ed) is Hebrew for "witness." This bilingual doubling signals the ethnic and linguistic boundary being established: Jacob the Hebrew and Laban the Aramean each name the boundary marker in their own tongue. The formal translation retains both proper names in their transliterated forms — "Jegar-sahadutha" and "Galeed" — following the established convention of formal English translations. The LXX renders Βουνὸς τῆς μαρτυρίας (Bounos tēs martyrias — "the Hill of Witness") for Galeed only, omitting the Aramaic name, a significant difference from the MT.

**13. "Mizpah" — הַמִּצְפָּה (v. 49)**

The name Mizpah (מִצְפָּה, Mitspah) derives from the root צָפָה (tsaphah — "to watch, to look out, to keep watch"). The narrator's explanation — "for he said, 'The LORD watch between you and me, when we are hidden from one another'" — makes the etymology transparent. The Hebrew verb יִצֶף (yitseph — "may he watch") is the direct play on Mitspah. The Mizpah saying in verse 49 ("The LORD watch between you and me") has become one of the most widely quoted verses in the Hebrew Bible in Christian popular usage, where it is often employed as a benediction for parting friends — though in its original context it is specifically a covenant-enforcement formula: God as the divine witness who watches over the agreement when the parties are separated from each other. The formal translation renders the clause precisely: "The LORD watch between you and me, when we are hidden from one another."

**14. "The God of Abraham and the God of Nahor, the God of their father" — plural verb (v. 53)**

Verse 53 contains a striking theological formulation: "The God of Abraham and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge between us" (יִשְׁפְּטוּ אֱלֹהִים בֵּינֵינוּ, yishpetu Elohim beinenu). The verb יִשְׁפְּטוּ (yishpetu) is grammatically plural — it agrees with Elohim in its plural form rather than the singular יִשְׁפֹּט (yishpot). This is unusual: in the vast majority of biblical texts, Elohim takes a singular verb when referring to the God of Israel. The plural verb here may reflect the sentence's rhetorical context — Laban's oath invokes three divine referents (God of Abraham, God of Nahor, God of their father) and the plural verb may be attracted to the compound subject, or it may reflect Laban's own polytheistic framework, where "gods" is genuinely plural. Jacob's response — he swears not by "the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor" but solely "by the Fear of his father Isaac" — implicitly corrects Laban's theology. The formal translation renders "judge between us" — a plural English verb that preserves the grammatical plurality of the Hebrew without forcing a theological decision. The LXX renders κρίναι ὁ θεὸς ἀνὰ μέσον ἡμῶν (krinai ho theos ana meson hēmōn — "may God judge between us"), using a singular verb and singular "God," harmonizing toward monotheistic orthodoxy and losing the grammatical plurality of the MT.

**15. "Offered a sacrifice on the mountain" — וַיִּזְבַּח יַעֲקֹב זֶבַח בָּהָר (v. 54)**

Jacob's covenant-sealing act is described with the verb זָבַח (zavakh — "to sacrifice, to slaughter a sacrificial offering"). The noun זֶבַח (zevakh) denotes a sacrificial slaughter in which the animal is both offered to God and then eaten by the participants. The following clause — "and called his kinsmen to eat bread" (וַיִּקְרָא לְאֶחָיו לֶאֱכָל לָחֶם) — describes the covenant meal that follows the sacrifice. The formal translation renders "offered a sacrifice on the mountain" followed by "called his kinsmen to eat bread," preserving both elements. The phrase "eat bread" (akhal lakhem) in covenantal contexts in the Hebrew Bible refers to the shared sacred meal that seals the agreement (cf. Exodus 18:12; 24:11). The LXX renders ἔθυσεν Ιακωβ θυσίαν ἐν τῷ ὄρει (ethysen Iakob thysian en tō orei — "Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain"), consistent with the MT.

**16. Verse 55 — "Early in the morning" / kissing and blessing**

The final verse (31:55 in Hebrew versification, which is 32:1 in some versification systems including the LXX) describes Laban's early-morning farewell: he kisses his grandchildren (בָּנָיו, banav — "his sons/grandchildren," here understood contextually as grandchildren since Jacob's children are in view) and his daughters (בְּנֹתָיו, benotav) and blesses them. The formal translation renders "grandchildren" since Jacob's children are Laban's grandchildren. Some formal translations render this "sons" to match the Hebrew banav strictly, but contextually the referents are clearly Jacob and Leah/Rachel's children. The LXX renders τοὺς υἱοὺς αὐτοῦ καὶ τὰς θυγατέρας αὐτοῦ (tous huious autou kai tas thygateras autou — "his sons and his daughters"), preserving the literal Hebrew rather than inferring "grandchildren."

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 31

Pages 27–29 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text across the three pages was legible. Key variants observed:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 31:7 | עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים ("ten times") | δέκα ἀμνάδας ("ten female lambs") | "ten times" | LXX reads a strikingly different text; MT well supported; formal translation follows MT |
| 31:13 | הָאֵל בֵּית אֵל ("the God of Bethel") | ὁ θεὸς ὁ ὀφθείς σοι ("the God who appeared to you") | "the God of Bethel" | LXX expands with relative clause; formal translation follows MT's terse form |
| 31:19 | הַתְּרָפִים ("the teraphim") | τὰ εἴδωλα ("the idols") | "the teraphim" | LXX interprets; formal translation retains the Hebrew technical term |
| 31:20 | לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי ("Laban the Aramean") | Λαβαν τὸν Σύρον ("Laban the Syrian") | "Laban the Aramean" | LXX renders Aramean as Syrian throughout; formal translation follows MT |
| 31:24 | מִטּוֹב עַד רָע ("from good to bad") | ἀπὸ τοῦ καλοῦ ἕως τοῦ πονηροῦ ("from the good to the evil") | "either good or bad" | MT and LXX agree structurally; formal translation renders the merism idiomatically |
| 31:47 | יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא ... גַּלְעֵד (both names preserved) | Βουνὸς τῆς μαρτυρίας (only one name, "Hill of Witness") | both names preserved | LXX omits the bilingual doubling; MT's bilingual naming is a significant narrative feature; formal translation follows MT |
| 31:53 | יִשְׁפְּטוּ (plural verb — "they shall judge") | κρίναι ὁ θεός (singular — "may God judge") | "judge between us" | LXX harmonizes to singular; MT plural verb is a textually significant reading; formal translation preserves the plural verb |
| 31:55 | בָּנָיו ("his sons/grandchildren") | τοὺς υἱοὺς αὐτοῦ ("his sons") | "his grandchildren" | LXX follows the Hebrew form literally; formal translation follows contextual referent (Jacob's children = Laban's grandchildren) |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Structure of Genesis 31: A Journey of Departure and Covenant**

Genesis 31 is structured in three movements of roughly equal length: (1) the departure from Paddan-aram (vv. 1–21), (2) the pursuit and confrontation (vv. 22–42), and (3) the covenant and farewell (vv. 43–55). Each movement turns on a divine intervention: the LORD commands Jacob to return (v. 3); God warns Laban in a dream (v. 24); Jacob invokes God as the unseen witness to the covenant (vv. 49–53). The chapter frames Jacob's entire departure as divinely orchestrated, not merely humanly engineered.

**The Teraphim: A Narrative Gap**

The episode of Rachel's theft of the teraphim (v. 19) is one of the most discussed narrative gaps in Genesis. The text does not explain Rachel's motive. She does not tell Jacob she has them (v. 32 — Jacob does not know), and they disappear from the narrative after the tent search without resolution. The reader is left with an unexplained act. Possible readings: (a) Rachel sought a protective household deity for the journey; (b) possession of household gods carried legal weight in the Hurrian/Nuzi legal context (though this theory is now contested); (c) Rachel's action is a form of symbolic severance from her father's household cult. The formal translation makes no interpretive decision — it narrates the theft (v. 19), Laban's accusation (v. 30), Jacob's unwitting death sentence (v. 32), Rachel's concealment (v. 34), and her excuse (v. 35) in sequence, leaving the gap intact as the text leaves it.

**Jacob's Self-Defense (vv. 36–42): The Covenant of Service Rendered**

Jacob's extended speech in verses 36–42 is one of the great passionate speeches of Genesis — an enumeration of twenty years' faithful service, cataloguing the conditions of his labor: heat by day, cold by night, the tearing of livestock, the changing of wages. The rhetorical force depends on the accumulation of detail. The formal translation renders each element precisely, preserving the list structure. The climax of the speech is verse 42: "If the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac, had not been with me, surely now you would have sent me away empty-handed." The Hebrew is emphatic: רֵיקָם שִׁלַּחְתָּנִי (reyqam shillakhtani — "empty-handed you would have sent me away"). The Fear of Isaac appears here for the first time — the God whose awesome presence has been Jacob's advocate.

**The Mizpah as Boundary, Not Benediction**

The Mizpah saying of verse 49 — "The LORD watch between you and me, when we are hidden from one another" — functions in its original context as a covenant-enforcement clause, not a blessing for departing friends. The LORD is invoked as the divine watcher who enforces the treaty terms when the human parties are out of each other's sight. The formal translation renders the verse precisely as it stands, without theological gloss. The reader who knows only the popular devotional use of "Mizpah" will encounter here the phrase's actual covenantal context.

**The God of Abraham and the God of Nahor**

Laban's oath in verse 53 is unique in the patriarchal narratives: it invokes not only the God of Abraham but also "the God of Nahor, the God of their father" — appealing to a shared ancestral deity whom both men's family lines honored. Whether this represents a genuine theological equivalence (Laban regards his god as the same deity Jacob worships) or a diplomatic formula (invoking each party's patron deity) is left open by the text. Jacob's pointed refusal to swear by Nahor's God — he swears only by "the Fear of his father Isaac" — draws the distinction that Laban's oath had blurred.
